summary: DeVault argues that DuckDuckGo, despite its privacy reputation, fails on three counts: it's not open source, it's merely a frontend for Bing's results, and its search quality is poor. He proposes building a fully FOSS search engine with its own crawler, using a centralized but open-source model similar to SourceHut rather than attempting difficult decentralization. The design would use a tiered whitelist approach, crawling authoritative domains first and expanding outward. He envisions the software enabling domain-specific search engines and supporting alternative internet protocols. Unable to identify an ethical monetization model, he pledges to eventually fund it as a public benefit project through SourceHut revenue.
key_points: DuckDuckGo is proprietary, not a real search engine (just a Bing frontend), and delivers poor results buried under content scrapers and blogspam
Existing FOSS alternatives like searX and YaCy each have fundamental flaws — searX also proxies others' results, while YaCy's decentralization makes it slow and low-quality
A tiered whitelist crawling approach would prioritize authoritative sources and let anyone spin up domain-specific search engines from the same software stack
The search engine should boldly support non-HTTP protocols like Gemini, man pages, Tor, and alternative name systems
No ethical monetization model exists for search engines, so DeVault would fund it as a public benefit project if SourceHut revenue grows enough
money_quotes: DuckDuckGo is not a search engine. It's more aptly described as a search engine frontend.
The authoritative sources for anything I want to find are almost always buried beneath 2-5 results from content scrapers and blogspam.
We need a real, working FOSS search engine, complete with its own crawler.
YaCy-style decentralization is way too hard to get right, especially when a search engine project already has a lot of Very Hard problems to solve.
There's a ton of potential in this domain which is just sitting on the floor right now.
I am not aware of any monetization approach for a search engine which squares with my ethics and doesn't fundamentally undermine the mission.
Advertisements or paid results are not going to fly — conflict of interest.
So, if no one else has figured it out by the time we have the resources to take it on, we'll do it.
key_insight: The internet desperately needs a fully open-source search engine with its own crawler, but building one requires pragmatic centralization and ethical funding — not ads, not decentralization theater.
